quoteFirst: The 3rd Division took over our front during the night of the 22nd and attacked at
quoteLast: the next day. Our guns helped to support the attack, which resulted in the capture of Romeries and Vertain. Continuing their victorious progress on the 24th, the 3rd Division captured Escarmain, and reached the railway line north of Ruesnes.

quoteFirst: By half-past twelve they were clear of the harbour. In a couple of hours they expected to pick up the destroyer _Kennet_.  By
quoteLast: there ought to be enough light to see a mile and a half ahead, and by that time they hoped to be close in to the mouth of the creek.  By half-past four the job might be over—should be finished—and they ought to be on the way home, with the Turkish patrol-boat in tow.
title: A Naval Venture: The War Story of an Armoured Cruiser
author: Jeans, T. T. (Thomas Tendron); Gillett, Frank, 1874-1927 [Illustrator]

quoteLast: the Germans put down a heavy barrage of gas and H.E. shells of all calibres on the Bailleul-Willerval line and the support line. At 4 a.m. the barrage increased over the whole of the front-line system and our posts were heavily bombarded with trench mortars.
title: The 56th Division (1st London Territorial Division)

quoteLast: a second record was made. Before half-past three the man was gone, and the camera winked every ten minutes until six o'clock quite in vain. I came down early this morning and got the roll of negatives.

quoteFirst: As I was sensible that the next three days would call for great physical exertion and constant wakefulness, I endeavored to get all the sleep I could on the night of the 11th. About 4 A.M. on the 12th, I was awakened by some one groping about my room in the dark and calling out my name. It proved to be Anderson, who came to announce to me that he had just received a dispatch from Beauregard, dated
quoteLast: , to the effect that he should open fire upon us in an hour. Finding it was determined not to return the fire until after breakfast, I remained in bed.
title: Reminiscences of Forts Sumter and Moultrie in 1860-'61
author: Doubleday, Abner, 1819-1893
